insights: Incidents are uniformly distributed across all five categories, with exactly 100 incidents per category—indicating no concentration, skew, or operational imbalance in category-level assignment.

Assignment distribution is perfectly balanced not only by category but also across agents, assignment groups, locations, and priority levels—ruling out structural bottlenecks or resource mismatches in any of these dimensions.
The absence of missing values and full datetime parseability confirms data integrity and eliminates data quality issues as a potential explanation for perceived imbalances.

The uniformity observed across all key assignment dimensions suggests that current routing or assignment logic operates with deterministic consistency—neither introducing nor amplifying variability in distribution.
summary: The analysis reveals no discrepancy or imbalance in incident distribution across categories or any related assignment dimension—the dataset shows perfect uniformity (100 incidents per category, and equally balanced assignments across agents, groups, locations, and priorities). This indicates that the underlying assignment mechanism functions with high consistency and does not introduce operational skew, concentration risk, or service pressure differentials. Data quality is confirmed robust, with no missing or malformed fields affecting distribution. Given the absence of imbalance, the focus should shift to validating whether this uniformity reflects actual operational reality—or whether upstream process controls, synthetic data generation, or routing constraints may be artificially enforcing this pattern.
